utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is significantly more effective than wo_skills. The wi_skills run (with the anon-skill-001 skill) scored 7/7 on the evaluation items, while wo_skills scored 5/7, failing J3 (GC not itemized by duration-based line items, presented as a single lump sum) and J4 (no traceable Seattle RSMeans location factor or midpoint escalation multiplier in the markup stack). utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is meaningfully higher quality than wo_skills on the most commercially critical evaluation item (J4). The key differentiator is change order markup structure: wi_skills correctly separates overhead and profit as distinct tiered line items (the construction industry standard for public contract change orders), while wo_skills applies a single blended 15% O&P figure — a commercially dangerous practice that obscures the fee structure and fails the explicit evaluation criterion. On all other items (J1–J3, J5–J7), both runs produce competent, structured outputs. However, wi_skills consistently shows greater numerical rigor: the bid/no-bid scorecard uses explicit weight multipliers and point totals (76/110), while wo_skills uses a qualitative risk-level table without numeric weights. The wi_skills output is also notably larger (23,757 bytes vs. 17,450 bytes for prebid_strategy.md; 8,498 vs. 7,635 for the CO), reflecting greater analytical depth. The skill's primary value-add is in the change order pricing methodology, which is precisely where commercially dangerous omissions are most likely to occur in practice.

utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, comprehensive bid estimate packages that pass all 7 evaluation criteria. However, wi_skills is more effective for the following reasons: (1) The skill-guided run produced a more methodologically transparent output with an explicit quality scoring rubric (78/100) and formal estimate classification framework. (2) The wi_skills markup stack is fully mathematically traceable with computed intermediate values verified via script (Step 6-7), while wo_skills markup computation is less explicitly verified. (3) The wi_skills QTO correction table is more structured and systematic, explicitly showing 'Junior Takeoff' vs 'Estimator Adjustment' columns with bases. (4) The skill framework prompted a more rigorous phase-by-phase approach aligned with professional estimating standards. While both outputs are bid-committee ready and cover all required evaluation items, the wi_skills output demonstrates more internal consistency, mathematical traceability, and systematic methodology — precisely the differentiators the test was designed to detect. The wo_skills agent performed surprisingly well (better than the expected baseline), likely due to the strong underlying LLM capabilities, but the skill provided meaningful structure and discipline that is evident in the output quality.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is significantly more effective than wo_skills across the commercially critical evaluation items. wi_skills produces a structured 9-factor weighted scorecard (J1: pass vs. fail), explicit per-quote red-flag identification for electrical subcontractors (J2: pass vs. fail), quantified VE proposals with savings and impact notes (J3: pass vs. fail), tiered markup with overhead and profit as separate line items in the change order (J4: pass vs. fail), and a calibrated numeric O&P range referenced to Dallas public market conditions (J5: pass vs. fail). Both runs pass J6 (Davis-Bacon and compliance flags) and J7 (schedule impact in change order). The skill-driven run transforms what would otherwise be a well-written but commercially dangerous narrative document into a structured, numerically traceable, multi-factor decision package. The key differentiator is the skill's enforcement of structured analytical frameworks — weighted scorecards, red-flag checklists, tiered markup tiers — that the baseline agent replaces with coherent but qualitative prose, which in a real bid scenario could lead to scope disputes, an under-priced change order, and a poorly calibrated bid fee.

utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, comprehensive deliverables that satisfied most evaluation criteria. The key differentiator is: (1) wi_skills invoked the anon-skill-001 skill explicitly (Step 1 of trajectory), while wo_skills did not use any skill; (2) For J6 (Houston location factor), neither run explicitly applied a numeric RSMeans location factor as a multiplier — both discussed Houston pricing qualitatively; (3) For J7 (contingency range), wo_skills more clearly met the 15–25% threshold with an explicit '15% to 20%' statement, while wi_skills split contingency into design (10–12%) + construction (4–6%) components that together straddle the threshold but the design-stage component alone is below 15%. Overall, both runs are surprisingly close in quality, with wo_skills actually performing slightly better on J5 (explicit accuracy band) and J7 (contingency range). The wi_skills run benefits from the skill's structured EVM framework and produced a cleaner EAC projection narrative, but the wo_skills run independently computed the same EVM metrics and produced a more detailed risk-contingency comparison. The skill provided structural guidance but did not produce a decisive quality advantage in the final output for this particular test scenario. wi_skills is marginally more effective due to explicit skill invocation and structured EVM presentation, but the gap is narrow.
